category_title: Active science leaders who connect school content with home-based animal care narratives
minimal_context: The student is participating in a Lunchtime Science group discussion about asthma using science texts.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Let's start our group discussion about asthma using these science texts.

STUDENT: I can explain it to the group. The little pipes or airways get blocked and, well, you get it when you take out a basketball.

TUTOR: Kalisha just suggested that might just be from running too hard. What is your response?

STUDENT: I was just adding an idea to what you said earlier, sharing facts from my science class and my personal stories.

TUTOR: What do you think about her point?

STUDENT: I get it once a year... It has gotten better... I don’t know. Kalisha is right! I don’t know if it is asthma or if I am out of shape. I don’t know any more.

options.A: Transition the decision as to when, and for how long, to take a quiet break from the instructor to the student during the group discussion.
options.B: Provide a two-pocket folder where the left side holds science texts to read and the right side holds discussion work completed by the end of the session.
options.C: Implement a progressive disciplinary sequence during the group discussion: first a warning, then a 10-minute timeout, followed by removal from the Lunchtime Science group.
options.D: Frame the science content as a substantive problem and encourage the student to ask questions, challenge ideas, pose solutions, and hypothesize during group discussions.
